name: Islamic Center of Irvine
mission: Mission statement - To establish a comprehensive center promoting Islamic values and reflecting excellence in serving the needs of the community
scoreSummary: Too early to rate numerically. This organization was founded in 2024 and is shown as an emerging organization while it builds a public track record.

evaluationSources: MUHSEN-Certified Organization (MUHSEN (Muslims Understanding & Helping Special Education Needs))

zakatClaimEvidence: ICOI's Community Assistance Program is dedicated to supporting those in need through a variety of essential services, including food distribution, clothing, tuition aid, and financial assistance. Your generosity helps us continue this vital work. If you'd like to make a difference and support our community, please click the button below to donate. Click here to donate Zakat Ul Maal. ... If you would like to contribute funds to help needy families you can do so by either donating your Zakat ul-Maal or your Sadaqah. ... 100% of the Islamic Center Of Irvine's Zakat fund goes towards providing zakat eligible members in the community. ICOI's fund helps needy individuals by providing a rent and utilities assistance program, food and other basic needs, assistance for stranded travelers, as well as covering the costs of mental health services and other healthcare for needy individuals. (Source: https://www.icoi.net/community-assistance/)

ui_signals_v1.recommendation_rationale: Early-stage organization. We show qualitative context while it builds a longer public track record.

keyConcerns.headline: Zakat-collecting organization holds 26 months of reserves
keyConcerns.detail: Zakat must be distributed promptly per Islamic jurisprudence. Large reserves raise concerns about timely distribution.

keyConcerns.headline: No outcome measurement or metrics tracking

components.evidence: Explicit zakat program (+4); Muslim-focused organization (+2); Islamic identity (+1)
components.improvement_suggestion: Clarify zakat allocation policy and eligible use categories for donors.
components.name: Muslim Donor Fit

alignment.rationale: Alignment 21/50: MEDIUM Muslim donor fit, religious cultural cause area

components.evidence: No beneficiary data available for cost-per-beneficiary scoring; high program ratio (≥85%) suggests efficient delivery
components.improvement_suggestion: Publish beneficiary counts consistently so cost-effectiveness can be assessed.
components.name: Cost Per Beneficiary

components.improvement_suggestion: If reserves are above policy target, document why and publish triggers for planned deployment.
components.name: Financial Health

components.improvement_suggestion: Document a clear theory of change with an explicit causal pathway.
components.name: Theory of Change

judge_issues.message: The rationale states "strong internal financial management and high program spending, balanced against a need for more robust external impact verification." While accurate, it could be more specific about the areas where impact verification is lacking, such as the absence of a documented theory of change and limited beneficiary data.

The rationale does not explicitly mention the risk deductions related to the organization holding 26 months of reserves while being a zakat-collecting organization. This is a key factor that affected the overall score and should be acknowledged.

dimension_explanations.alignment: With an alignment score of 21/50, the organization shows a medium fit for donors, supported by its ZAKAT-ELIGIBLE status and clear community assistance policies [2]. The score reflects a balance between the high urgency of religious and cultural preservation and the current level of public data confidence [3].
dimension_explanations.impact: The center demonstrates high operational capacity with a program expense ratio of 89.9% and holds 26.3 months of working capital to ensure long-term stability [1][3]. While financial health is strong, the impact score of 36/50 reflects a focus on capacity building where direct cost-per-beneficiary data is not fully available [1].

baseline_narrative.summary: The Islamic Center of Irvine Inc (ICOI) operates as a religious and community center in Southern California, reporting a total revenue of $2,209,994 [1][3]. The organization is recognized as ZAKAT-ELIGIBLE, maintaining a dedicated community assistance program to support those in need [2].

alignment.improvement: Clarifying the <cite id="9">Zakat allocation policy</cite> across different asnaf categories would improve alignment for specific Zakat donors.

credibility.improvement: To strengthen credibility, the organization should <cite id="15">upgrade to a Silver or Gold Candid seal</cite> by providing more detailed audited financial information.

ideal_donor_profile.best_for_summary: Best for donors who want to support the long-term growth of Islamic community infrastructure in Southern California through a financially stable and Zakat-eligible institution.
donor_motivations: Want to fulfill Zakat obligations through local community assistance
Value high financial efficiency and low fundraising overhead
Prefer supporting established institutions with significant assets
Interested in capacity building for local Muslim organizations
giving_considerations: Focus is primarily regional rather than international
High reserves suggest the organization is not in an immediate cash crisis
Impact is measured through service availability rather than RCT-level data
ideal_donor_profile.not_ideal_for: Donors seeking high-frequency international emergency relief or those requiring rigorous academic impact evaluations.

impact_evidence.evidence_grade_explanation: Grade C reflects strong financial reporting and verified Zakat status but a lack of specific outcome metrics.
impact_evidence.theory_of_change_summary: ICOI creates impact by providing religious services and financial assistance to the local community, though a formal causal pathway is not documented.
impact_evidence.why_evidence_matters: Evidence ensures that community assistance programs are effectively moving beneficiaries toward self-sufficiency.

long_term_outlook.room_for_funding_explanation: While the organization has high reserves, its steady revenue growth and expanding grantmaking suggest capacity for new projects.

strengths.detail: The organization directs <cite id="3">89.9% of its total expenses</cite> toward its core programs and services, significantly exceeding standard efficiency benchmarks.
ICOI maintains <cite id="10">26.3 months of working capital</cite>, providing a strong safety net for long-term organizational sustainability.
The charity reports a <cite id="4">fundraising efficiency of $0.00 per $1 raised</cite>, ensuring that donor contributions are not diluted by marketing costs.

strengths_deep_dive: ICOI's financial health is underscored by a <cite id="11">3-year revenue CAGR of 10.6%</cite>, reflecting steady growth in community support. This growth is managed by a <cite id="12">board of 9 independent members</cite>, ensuring strong oversight. Their grantmaking capacity is particularly notable, with <cite id="5">over $2.1 million in domestic grants</cite> issued in the most recent tax year, primarily supporting local infrastructure like the <cite id="6">Rahma Center</cite>.
rich_narrative.summary: The Islamic Center of Irvine Inc (ICOI) serves as a vital community hub in Southern California, dedicated to <cite id="1">promoting Islamic values and excellence in service</cite>. Operating with a <cite id="2">total revenue of $2,209,994</cite>, the organization functions as both a religious institution and a significant social service provider. A defining characteristic of ICOI is its high operational efficiency, maintaining a <cite id="3">program expense ratio of 89.9%</cite>, which ensures the vast majority of funds are directed toward core community services rather than administrative overhead. Furthermore, the organization demonstrates <cite id="4">exceptional fundraising efficiency of $0.00 per $1 raised</cite>, indicating that its development efforts are largely self-sustaining or volunteer-driven. Beyond its local religious services, ICOI acts as a major grantmaker, distributing <cite id="5">$2,125,949 in total grants</cite> to domestic recipients, including substantial support for the <cite id="6">Rahma Center</cite>. The organization is <cite id="7">Zakat-eligible</cite> and maintains a structured <cite id="8">community assistance program</cite> that serves specific asnaf categories including the <cite id="9">poor (fuqara), needy (masakin), and those in debt (gharimin)</cite>. With <cite id="10">26.3 months of working capital</cite> in reserve, ICOI exhibits a high degree of financial stability, allowing it to maintain consistent operations despite economic fluctuations.
